content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN


FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION


March 7, 1996

Ms. Fran Roudebush
697 Ridge Street
Portola, California 96122

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-071

Dear Ms. Roudebush:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
responsibilities as a candidate under the campaign provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

You are a member of the Chamber of Commerce and are thus
entitled to use the Chamber's bulk mail permit; you used this
permit to send a campaign mailing in support of your campaign for
county supervisor. Should the use of the bulk mail permit be
reported as a contribution from yourself to your campaign or from
the Chamber to your campaign?

CONCLUSION

The use of the bulk mail permit was an in-kind contribution
from you to your campaign committee and should be reported as
such.

FACTS

Last month, you joined your local Chamber of Commerce. You
paid the membership fees out of your personal funds. One of the
privileges of membership is that any member is allowed to use the
Chamber of Commerce's bulk mail permit. On approximately January

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-071
Page 2


26, 1996, you and the Committee to Re-Elect Fran Roudebush mailed
out 230 requests for funds using the Chamber's bulk mail permit.
The committee paid the postage fees of 18 cents per piece.

ANALYSIS

Section 82015 provides that the term "contribution" means "a
payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third
party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it
is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made
for political purposes."  The term "contribution" includes the
granting of discounts not extended to the public generally.
(Section 82015.)  A payment made for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for the
nomination of a candidate or payments made at the behest of a
candidate is made for "political purposes." (Regulation
18215(a).)  As a result, a candidate committee's use of another
person's discounted bulk mail permit is an in-kind contribution.

You are an individual entitled to use the Chamber's bulk mail
permit by virtue of your membership in the Chamber and you have
asked whether the use of the permit is a discount from the Chamber
or from yourself to your campaign.  In the Hagar Advice Letter,
No. A-94-334, we addressed a similar situation.  A longtime
customer of newspaper advertising services had a contractual
advertising rate much lower than the rate the newspaper charged
one time or infrequent customers.  The longtime customer then in
turn contributed his discounted advertising to a political
candidate, thereby enabling the candidate to obtain advertising at
a rate substantially lower than the rates offered to other
candidates.

The Hagar Letter concluded that there was no reportable
contribution from the newspaper to the candidate.  The discount
offered by the newspaper was provided pursuant to a contractual
arrangement with a longtime customer and was not offered for
political purposes.  The letter further concluded that any
contribution that did result had been made from the longtime
customer to the candidate, not from the newspaper to the
candidate.

Similarly, in your situation, the bulk mail permit discount
was not offered for political purposes.  The Chamber makes the
permit available to all dues paying members of the Chamber as part
of the perquisites of Chamber membership.  When you used the
discounted mail to send out a campaign mailing, you made a
contribution to the campaign committee, but the Chamber made no

File No. A-96-071
Page 3


contribution.2

Accordingly, the use of the permit must be reported as a
contribution from you to your campaign committee. The Commission
has advised that the fair market value is the difference between
the bulk mail postage rate and the regular postage rate the
candidate would be required to pay. (Wewer Advice Letter, No. I-
91-531, Alsop Advice Letter, No. I-91-499, Yu Advice Letter, No.
I-86-25.) However, because a bulk mail permit is readily
obtainable by the general public, including candidates and
committees, the price of the same or similar bulk mail permit may
also be used for purposes of determining the fair market value of
the contribution. (Olson Advice Letter, No. I-93-230.)
Therefore, if you used a bulk mail permit readily obtainable by
the general public, you may report the amount of the contribution
as: (1) the cost of obtaining the same bulk mail permit; or (2)
the difference between the bulk mail postage rate and the regular
first class postage rate. (Olson Advice Letter, supra.)

On the other hand, if the permit you used is a permit not
readily obtainable by the general public, such as a nonprofit
organization permit, you must report: (1) the difference between
the standard bulk mail postage rate and the nonprofit bulk mail
postage rate, plus the cost of a bulk mail permit; or (2) the
difference between the nonprofit rate and the first class rate.
For large mailings, the former will, of course, result in a
smaller reportable contribution.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Liane Randolph
Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak


2 You have also stated that the Chamber receives funds from
Plumas Corporation, which receives 50% of the temporary occupancy
tax in the county. Because we have determined that the Chamber
did not make a contribution to your campaign, there is no
Political Reform Act issue with regard to misuse of public funds.
